title: Multiple Malicious DNS Resolutions
detectionType: DNS / Umbrella — bulk resolutions in risky categories
description: A host or user generated a high volume (e.g. 25+ within one hour) of DNS resolutions falling into risky categories — Malware, Command-and-Control, Botnet, Newly Seen Domains, or Phishing. The intent is to catch threats that endpoint detection may have missed (a payload that evaded CrowdStrike but still reaches out to threat-intel-known or newly-seen infrastructure), while acknowledging that bulk risky resolutions are frequently benign (ad/analytics/CDN sprawl, a single page loading many trackers, newly-registered legitimate domains). The core of triage is attributing the resolutions to an originating process, which requires cross-referencing Umbrella (what was resolved) with the endpoint detection platform (what process resolved it) and identity logs where a user/egress IP is involved. A fallback path handles cases where the originating system is not managed by the endpoint platform.

intake.title: Intake and capture the resolution set
intake.instructions:
Confirm this is a new alert (not a duplicate) and capture what the DNS layer actually saw.

- Record the identifier Umbrella attributes the activity to: internal host, roaming client, network/egress public IP, or a username if tied to a directory.
- Export the list of flagged resolutions with their categories, timestamps, and counts per category.
- Note the category mix: is it dominated by Newly Seen Domains, by Malware/C2/Botnet, by Phishing, or a blend.
- Note the time window and whether the resolutions are clustered (a burst) or spread evenly (possible beaconing).
- Record the reporting source and alert time.

evidence.label: Umbrella attribution (host / roaming client / egress IP / user)

triage-domains.title: Enrich and separate the domains
triage-domains.instructions:
Not all flagged domains carry equal weight. Enrich them before touching the host.

- For each distinctive domain, gather reputation and any known campaign/malware/phishing affiliation from a threat-intel capability.
- Separate the set into buckets: (a) credible malicious infrastructure (known C2/malware/botnet or convincing phishing), (b) newly-seen/low-info domains with no clear verdict, and (c) obvious ad/analytics/tracker/CDN or otherwise benign-but-noisy domains.
- For Newly Seen Domains, check registration age and whether they resemble legitimate new services vs. algorithmically-generated names (DGA-like).
- Note whether any domain is a lookalike/typosquat of a brand your users would trust (phishing signal).

host-identified?.title: Can you identify the specific originating host/endpoint?
host-identified?.instructions: Umbrella may attribute activity to a specific internal host, or only to a network egress/public IP (many hosts behind NAT) or an off-network roaming identifier. You must know the endpoint before you can attribute a process.

options.label: No — only an egress IP or ambiguous attribution

identify-host.title: Resolve the originating host behind the egress
identify-host.instructions:
Attribution is only to a public/egress IP or a roaming client. Narrow it to a specific system and, where relevant, a user.

- Correlate the Umbrella timestamps against internal DHCP/DNS/NAT/proxy logs to map the egress IP to an internal host at that moment.
- If the resolutions are tied to a user, review identity logs (Okta) for sessions active from that egress IP in the window, to associate a person and their device.
- Consider roaming/off-network clients: an Umbrella roaming client ID or device posture record may name the endpoint directly.
- If multiple hosts sit behind the egress IP, use the resolved-domain timing to narrow which host(s) generated the flagged lookups.

evidence.label: Egress IP → internal host mapping (source & time)
Okta sessions from egress IP in window (user/device)

host-resolved?.title: Was a specific host successfully identified?
host-resolved?.instructions: Decide whether correlation gave you an actual endpoint to investigate, or whether attribution remains to an IP only.

options.label: No — cannot resolve to a single host

in-edr?.title: Is the identified host managed by the endpoint detection platform (CrowdStrike)?
in-edr?.instructions: This determines whether process-level attribution is even possible. Managed workstations/servers with the sensor allow you to tie a DNS resolution to a process. Unmanaged, BYOD, IoT/OT, printers, or guest devices do not.

options.label: Yes — host has the sensor
options.note: Pivot to the endpoint platform for process attribution.

options.label: No — unmanaged / BYOD / IoT / no sensor
options.note: No process attribution; use network + user evidence.

edr-process.title: Attribute the resolutions to a process in the endpoint platform
edr-process.instructions:
Pivot from Umbrella (what was resolved) to the endpoint platform (what resolved it). This is the decisive step.

- Search the host's endpoint telemetry around the Umbrella timestamps for DNS request / network events matching the flagged domains.
- Identify the initiating process for the resolutions: a browser or Explorer/shell, a known application, or an unexpected binary (script host, LOLBin, unsigned/renamed executable, something in a user-writable/temp path).
- For any suspicious process, capture its path, signature, parent process, and reputation, and note whether the endpoint platform itself raised any related (possibly lower-severity or suppressed) detections.
- Determine whether the resolutions map to user-driven browsing (many domains loaded by one page) or to autonomous process activity (periodic callbacks independent of user action).

process-verdict?.title: What does the initiating process indicate?
process-verdict?.instructions: Combine the process identity with the domain buckets. An unexpected/unsigned binary or autonomous callbacks to malicious infrastructure indicates a missed infection. A browser resolving the domains points to user activity — which is either a phishing click or benign browsing depending on the domains. If the process itself looks fine but resolved credible malicious domains, keep it as suspicious and investigate the malicious branch.

options.label: Suspicious process / autonomous callbacks to bad infra
options.note: Likely payload that evaded the endpoint platform.

options.label: Browser/user resolved credible PHISHING domains
options.note: Possible phishing click; check email & credentials.

no-edr-branch.title: Investigate without endpoint process attribution
no-edr-branch.instructions:
The host has no sensor, so you cannot see the initiating process. Build the case from network, DNS, and user evidence instead.

- Characterize the device: what it is (BYOD laptop, phone, IoT/OT, printer, server), who owns/uses it, and what it is normally expected to do on the network.
- Analyze the DNS pattern itself: periodic/beaconing cadence and DGA-like names suggest automated malware; a burst of mixed ad/tracker domains suggests browsing.
- Gather any available network evidence: firewall/proxy/NetFlow showing whether connections actually followed the resolutions, to which destinations, and data volume.
- If a user is associated, review identity logs (Okta) for suspicious authentication around the window and interview the user about their activity.

no-edr-verdict?.title: What does the network/DNS/user evidence indicate?
no-edr-verdict?.instructions: Without process attribution, weigh the strongest available signals: pattern shape, follow-through connections, domain reputation, and user account. Err toward caution when malicious infrastructure was contacted and connections followed.

options.label: Automated callbacks to malicious infra — likely infection
options.note: Treat as compromised despite no sensor.

no-host-branch.title: Handle unresolved attribution
no-host-branch.instructions:
You cannot pin the activity to a specific host, or you lack the visibility to reach a verdict. Do not simply close — the alert may reflect a real infection you cannot yet see.

- Document exactly what attribution you DO have (egress IP, network segment, time window, user candidates).
- Take proportionate protective action: block/sinkhole the credible malicious domains at the DNS/network layer so any affected host is denied resolution while you investigate.
- Note what visibility gap prevented attribution (unmanaged segment, NAT with insufficient logging, missing sensor coverage) as a coverage improvement item.
- Decide whether the malicious-domain evidence is strong enough to escalate for broader hunting, or whether it can be monitored.

no-host-verdict?.title: Is the malicious-domain evidence strong enough to escalate?
no-host-verdict?.instructions: Even without a host, credible contact with known-bad infrastructure may justify escalation for broader hunting; weak/benign evidence can be monitored and closed with a coverage note.

options.label: Strong — credible malicious infra contacted
options.note: Escalate for hunting despite no host.

malware-confirm.title: Confirm and characterize the suspected infection
malware-confirm.instructions:
Evidence points to malware that endpoint detection missed or that is running on an unmonitored host. Pin down what you are dealing with before containment.

- Confirm the malicious nature: match the process/domains to a known family or campaign where possible, and confirm connections followed the resolutions (actual C2/beaconing, not just lookups).
- On a managed host, collect the payload details: file path(s), hash(es), persistence mechanism, and how it likely arrived (email, download, removable media, lateral).
- Establish the beaconing timeline: first callback to latest, and cadence.
- Preserve volatile evidence (running processes, network connections, memory artifacts) before remediation.

contain-host.title: Contain the host and infrastructure
contain-host.instructions:
Credible infection. Contain before scoping and handoff.

- Isolate the affected host from the network per policy while preserving investigative access; for an unmanaged host, use network-level isolation (switchport/NAC/segment block).
- Sinkhole or block the malicious domains and known C2 IPs at the DNS and network layers across the environment.
- Block the payload by hash on the endpoint platform where the host is managed.
- Record every containment action with timestamps.

scope-threat.title: Scope across the environment
scope-threat.instructions:
Determine how far the threat reached before handoff.

- Query the DNS layer (Umbrella) for any OTHER hosts resolving the same malicious domains — this is often the fastest fleet-wide scoping signal.
- On the endpoint platform, hunt the payload hash and related indicators across all managed hosts.
- Build a cross-host timeline and identify any lateral movement or additional affected users/accounts (review Okta for suspicious auth tied to affected users).
- Assess what data/systems the affected host(s) and account(s) could reach.

escalate-threat.title: Escalate to incident response
escalate-threat.instructions:
Confirmed (or strongly suspected) malicious activity that endpoint detection did not stop, with initial containment and scope established. Hand off with a complete package.

- Ensure attribution, domain enrichment, process/payload details, beaconing timeline, affected hosts/accounts, and containment actions are all recorded above.
- Assign incident severity per your matrix and notify the on-call IR owner.
- Confirm volatile evidence was preserved and DNS/identity logs retained before their retention windows expire.
- Note this as a potential endpoint-detection gap for post-incident review.
- Transfer ownership; eradication and recovery proceed under the IR playbook.
escalate-threat.summary: Bulk malicious DNS resolutions attributed to malware/C2 that endpoint detection missed; contained, scoped, and escalated to IR.

phishing-branch.title: Investigate the suspected phishing click
phishing-branch.instructions:
The resolutions include credible phishing domains reached via user/browser activity. Determine whether credentials were exposed.

- Correlate with email security: find the message(s) delivering the phishing link, who received them, and who clicked (delivery/click telemetry).
- Determine whether the user reached a credential-harvesting page and whether credentials were plausibly entered.
- Review identity logs (Okta) for the affected user around and after the click: successful logins from unusual IPs, new MFA enrollment, MFA fatigue, or session anomalies indicating the harvested credentials were used.
- Interview the user where feasible: did they enter credentials, approve an MFA prompt, or download anything.

phishing-verdict?.title: Were credentials exposed or the account compromised?
phishing-verdict?.instructions: If credentials were entered or Okta shows post-click account misuse, treat as an identity compromise. If the user clicked but did not submit credentials and there is no account misuse, it is a contained phishing exposure. If it was blocked or never engaged, it trends toward false positive / benign.

options.label: Clicked only, no credentials entered, no misuse
Blocked / not engaged — benign
options.note: No exposure; close as false positive.

contain-identity.title: Contain the compromised identity
contain-identity.instructions:
Phishing led to credential exposure or account misuse. Contain the identity before scoping and handoff.

- Revoke active sessions/tokens in the identity provider (Okta) and force re-authentication.
- Reset the password and require trusted MFA re-enrollment; remove any attacker-registered MFA factors.
- Reverse persistence on associated accounts: mailbox rules/forwarding, illicit OAuth grants, recovery-info changes.
- Block the phishing domains at the DNS/network layer and record every action with timestamps.

evidence.label: Rules / OAuth / recovery reversed; domains blocked

scope-identity.title: Scope the identity compromise
scope-identity.instructions:
Determine what the attacker reached with the credentials before handoff.

- Build a timeline of attacker authentication and activity from the identity logs (Okta) and downstream applications.
- Enumerate data accessed/exported and messages sent from the account.
- Check whether the same phishing campaign hit other users (email correlation) and whether other credentials were exposed.
- Identify any lateral access to other systems the account could reach.

escalate-identity.title: Escalate identity compromise to incident response
escalate-identity.instructions:
Confirmed credential/account compromise via phishing, with containment and scope established. Hand off with a complete package.

- Ensure user, phishing domains/email, credential-exposure evidence, attacker timeline, data exposure, and containment actions are all recorded above.
- Assign incident severity per your matrix and notify the on-call IR owner.
- Preserve identity and email logs before retention windows expire.
- Transfer ownership; eradication, notifications, and recovery proceed under the IR playbook.
escalate-identity.summary: Phishing click via flagged DNS resolutions led to identity compromise; account contained, scoped, and escalated to IR.

benign-browsing.title: Confirm benign browsing / adtech before closing
benign-browsing.instructions:
The resolutions look like ordinary browsing noise — many ad/analytics/tracker/CDN domains and newly-registered-but-legitimate services loaded by normal web activity. Confirm before closing.

- Verify the initiating process (where available) is a legitimate browser and that the resolutions coincide with genuine user browsing.
- Confirm none of the flagged domains carry credible malicious intel once enriched (newly-seen ≠ malicious).
- Confirm there is no beaconing cadence and no autonomous non-browser process involved.
- Note whether the volume is explained by a single content-heavy site or normal daily browsing.

evidence.label: No credible malicious intel on domains

benign-verdict?.title: Is this cleanly benign, or benign-but-policy-relevant?
benign-verdict?.instructions: Distinguish pure false positive from activity that is not malicious but violates acceptable-use (e.g. the user visited disreputable sites, used prohibited services) and may warrant a note to management/HR without being a security incident.

options.label: Cleanly benign — normal browsing/adtech
options.note: False positive; tune the noisy categories/domains.

options.label: Benign but policy-relevant activity
options.note: Not malicious; refer per acceptable-use policy.

close-fp.title: Close — false positive (benign resolutions)
close-fp.instructions:
The bulk resolutions are explained by benign activity (ordinary browsing, adtech/CDN sprawl, or newly-registered legitimate domains) with no malicious follow-through and no credential exposure.

- Document the benign explanation and the attributing evidence (process/browser, clean enrichment, no beaconing).
- Propose scoped tuning: raise thresholds or allow-list chronically noisy legitimate domains/categories so routine browsing does not re-alert.
- Note any category that is systematically over-firing (e.g. Newly Seen Domains) for detection engineering.
close-fp.summary: Bulk risky-category DNS resolutions explained by benign browsing/adtech/newly-registered domains; no malicious follow-through. Closed as false positive with tuning proposed.

close-policy.title: Close — benign but policy-relevant
close-policy.instructions:
Activity is not a security threat but violates acceptable-use (e.g. prohibited categories/services). No malware, C2, or credential exposure.

- Document the non-malicious nature and the specific policy concern.
- Refer to the appropriate owner (management/HR) per acceptable-use policy rather than treating as a security incident.
- Propose tuning so genuinely benign traffic of this shape does not generate security alerts.
close-policy.summary: Resolutions non-malicious but policy-relevant; referred per acceptable-use policy and tuning proposed.

phishing-exposure-close.title: Close — phishing exposure, no compromise
phishing-exposure-close.instructions:
The user reached a phishing domain but did not submit credentials and no account misuse is evident.

- Document that the link was clicked but no credentials were entered and identity logs show no misuse.
- Block the phishing domains at the DNS/network layer and confirm the delivering email is remediated for all recipients.
- Provide user awareness follow-up and confirm no other recipients engaged.
- Propose blocking/tuning so the campaign's infrastructure is denied going forward.
phishing-exposure-close.summary: Phishing link clicked but no credentials exposed and no account misuse; domains blocked, email remediated, user educated.

close-unresolved.title: Close — unattributed, protective action taken
close-unresolved.instructions:
Attribution to a specific host was not possible and the malicious-domain evidence was insufficient to sustain a full investigation, but protective steps were taken.

- Document the attribution available and the protective block/sinkhole applied to any credible malicious domains.
- Record the visibility gap (unmanaged segment, insufficient NAT/DHCP logging, missing sensor coverage) as a coverage-improvement item for follow-up.
- Note conditions that should trigger re-opening (repeat resolutions, new hosts hitting the same domains).
close-unresolved.summary: Bulk resolutions could not be attributed to a host; credible malicious domains blocked and a visibility-gap coverage item filed. Closed with monitoring.
